title: Physical Examination of the Healthy Newborn
authors: Griswold K, Srinivas GL / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเชี่ยวชาญ Physical Examination of the Newborn?
การตรวจร่างกายทารกแรกเกิดภายใน 24 ชั่วโมงหลังคลอดไม่ใช่แค่ 'ตรวจผ่าน' — มันคือ the first comprehensive assessment of transition to extrauterine life. (1) Critical CHD: 6–8/1000 live births; ductaldependent lesions silent ใน delivery room แต่ kill after ductus closes; (2) DDH: 1/1000 true DDH — Ortolani clunk ต้องเจ็บตัว train หลายครั้งจึงจะรู้สึกได้; (3) Congenital cataract: preventable cause of blindness — missed red reflex = missed window; (4) Brachial plexus palsy after shoulder dystocia: Erb (C5–C6) — waiter's tip; manage with PT vs neurosurgical referral; (5) Critical CHD screening: pulse oximetry protocol (AAP 2020) — SpO₂ <94% or >4% discordance R hand vs foot → workup. Neonatologist อยู่ใน front line ของการ detect anomaly ที่ prenatal US พลาด — ทักษะนี้ต้องคม.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย objectives และลำดับการตรวจร่างกายทารกแรกเกิด: timing, preparation, order of exam
ระบุ normal growth parameters ตาม GA: weight, OFC cut-offs; interpret Ballard score (6+6 criteria)
วินิจฉัยผื่น/birthmark ที่ benign (erythema toxicum, milia, dermal melanocytosis) vs ที่ต้อง investigate (café-au-lait ≥3, port-wine stain ใกล้ V1–V2 → Sturge-Weber)
แยก caput succedaneum / cephalhematoma / subgaleal hemorrhage: anatomy, crossing sutures, urgency
ตรวจ red reflex อย่างถูกต้อง; ระบุ conditions ที่ทำให้ leukocoria
แยก innocent vs significant heart murmur; อธิบาย limitations ของการตรวจ newborn CHD; pulse oximetry screening protocol AAP 2020
ประเมิน genitalia ทั้ง male (hypospadias, undescended testes, torsion) และ female (ambiguous genitalia, imperforate hymen)
ทำ Ortolani + Barlow maneuver สำหรับ DDH; ระบุ risk factors + screening US indication
ตรวจ neurologic exam: cranial nerves ทั้ง 12, tone assessment (pull-to-sit, axillary suspension, ventral suspension), primitive reflexes
อธิบาย health promotion objectives: breastfeeding, SIDS prevention, critical CHD screening, car seat challenge, vitamin K, hearing screen
1. Routine Newborn Examination — Framework
1.1 Timing and Objectives
Primary survey: in delivery room — brief look, no major anomaly, pink, breathing normal. Performer: OB or delivery nurse.
Routine examination: within 24 hours of birth by trained health care provider (AAP/ACOG Guidelines for Perinatal Care 8th ed 2017). Defer ≥1 hour for uninterrupted skin-to-skin + first breastfeed.
Objectives: assess extrauterine transition, screen congenital anomaly, identify neonatal problems, promote health, plan discharge, engage parents

Prevalence of Significant Congenital Anomalies — What to Look For
CHD: 6–8/1000 live births (only 0.8/1000 identified day 1); hypospadias: 4/1000; Down syndrome: 1.3/1000; talipes equinovarus: 1.1/1000; DDH: 0.7–1.1/1000 (7/1000 abnormal initial exam); cleft lip+palate: 0.8/1000; spina bifida/anencephaly: 0.5/1000. Prenatal US miss rate: hydronephrosis/urinary: 200–250/1000 detected prenatally — but many anomalies still found first at newborn exam. Always examine with anomalies in mind.
1.2 Chart Review Before Exam
Maternal: blood type/Rh/DAT, HIV, HBsAg, syphilis, GBS, GDM, hypertension, hypothyroid; prenatal US results, NIPT/amnio; labor: fever, ROM duration, amniotic fluid quality, operative delivery
Infant: sex, Apgar, birth weight/length/OFC, vital signs, oxygen, feeding, glucose; any discrepancy from stated GA
Family/social history: paternal family hx of hip dysplasia, phototherapy; maternal psychiatric/substance use; housing/food security; breastfeeding plans
1.3 Order of Examination

Flexible order — cover entire body. Strategy: (1) opportunistic exam while quiet: listen heart, examine eyes, check femoral pulses, gentle hip exam; (2) general observation while undressing; (3) remove diaper for genitalia; (4) prone for back/spine + tone
Always perform at mother's bedside; some elements can be done skin-to-skin. Do NOT disturb nursing dyad unnecessarily
2. Measurements and Growth Parameters
2.1 Weight
Plot on gestation-specific chart: Olsen or Fenton charts for GA-specific percentile. WHO/CDC charts = term only (40 wk) → misidentify late-preterm (34–36 wk) and postmature (>41 wk)
Normal 10th–90th perc at 40 wk: female 2.85–4.07 kg (mean 3.45 kg); male 2.9–4.23 kg (mean 3.58 kg)

Physiologic weight loss: median 7% by 48h. Concern if >10% or continued substantial loss after day 4–5. NEWT.org (Newborn Weight Loss Tool): percentile by mode of delivery, up to 30 days — adjunct for breastfeeding adequacy
2.2 OFC (Head Circumference)
Measure maximum OFC (inion to nasion) with disposable tape; plot on GA-specific chart. 10th–90th perc at 40 wk: female 32.6–36.2 cm; male 32.8–36.8 cm
OFC can change markedly in first days from molding — serial measurement more informative than single value
Anterior fontanelle normal size by region: Africa 3.15 cm, Americas 2.35 cm, Asia 2.49 cm, Europe 2.01 cm (pooled mean 2.58 cm, Oumer 2021). Much larger → Down syndrome, hypothyroid, chondrodysplasia
Tense fontanelle (not crying): elevated ICP or meningitis → cranial US ± LP. Sagittal suture often separated; coronal often overriding post-delivery.
2.3 Length
Measured prone with legs extended using length board. Rarely accurate without board → not reliable for short stature. Upper segment:lower segment ratio 1.7 = normal (helps screen skeletal dysplasia)
3. Skin Assessment
3.1 Color

Acrocyanosis: common first day — peripheral only; normal. Central cyanosis (tongue blue): urgent → check SpO₂ immediately. Blue discoloration of presenting part (face/breech) with pink tongue = peripheral venous congestion, not central cyanosis
Polycythemia (Hct >65%): may appear cyanotic despite adequate oxygenation (↑ reduced Hb). Plethora = red from polycythemia; pallor = anemia or shock → check Hb/Hct
Harlequin color change: longitudinal half-red/half-white demarcation; vasomotor instability; resolves in minutes; benign but alarms parents — reassure
Jaundice in first 24h: likely hemolytic → urgent investigation + treatment. May also indicate congenital infection.
3.2 Benign Neonatal Rashes — No Treatment Needed

rows: Whitish papules on erythematous base; day 2–3; fresh crops up to day 5
Eosinophils on microscopy; resolves by day 5; benign
Starts 37–38 wk, may be present at birth; pustules without erythema; wipe top → melanotic freckle
More common in Black infants; neutrophils (not bacterial); distinguish from sepsis
White cysts on nose/cheeks from keratin/sebaceous retention in pilosebaceous follicles
Blue-black macules at spine base/buttocks; present at birth; Black/Asian infants
May be misdiagnosed as bruise — DOCUMENT location/distribution at birth
Post-term infants; no investigation needed unless plate-like/deep (ichthyosis)

blocks.text: 3.3 Vascular Birthmarks — Malformations vs Tumors

Vascular Anomalies Classification (Wassef 2015) — Malformations vs Tumors
Malformations (present at birth, errors in vasculogenesis): (1) Nevus simplex/macular stain ('stork bite'): 40–60% infants; pink macules on eyelids/forehead/nape; fade first year except nape. (2) Port-wine stain (nevus flammeus): unilateral/segmental capillary malformation; does NOT fade; grows with child. If central+lateral forehead → Sturge-Weber risk (glaucoma + intracranial lesions + seizures); multiple V branches → early ophthalmology. (3) Klippel-Trénaunay: vascular malformation + lower extremity overgrowth. Tumors: Infantile hemangioma (strawberry nevus): NOT apparent at birth; appears weeks 1–3; grows 3–15 months; then involutes. Beard/nares → airway; midline spine → spinal canal. Topical propranolol if cosmetic concern; oral propranolol if airway/GI bleeding. Diffuse liver hemangiomas → hypothyroidism → monitor TFT.
Café-au-lait spots: common; ≥3 lesions or 1 large lesion → investigate neurofibromatosis type 1 (check axillae/groin for freckling) or McCune-Albright
4. Head, Face, Eyes, Ears, Mouth, Neck
4.1 Head — Scalp Swellings: Critical Differential

Caput Succedaneum vs Cephalhematoma vs Subgaleal Hemorrhage
Caput succedaneum: edema of dermal layers overlying presenting part. Crosses suture lines; pitting; resolves first week. Cephalhematoma: bleeding between periosteum and skull plate. Confined within suture lines; fluctuant; round; well-circumscribed. Usually overlies parietal bone. Resolves 2–3 weeks; occasional calcification → remodels over months. ↑ early jaundice. Subgaleal hemorrhage (EMERGENCY): bleeding between galea aponeurosis and periosteum. Vacuum extraction + coagulopathy = risk factors. Boggy head + 'wave sign' (fluid shifts across head) + anterior ear displacement. Large blood loss potential → hypovolemic shock. Early recognition + treatment paramount.

Craniosynostosis: premature fusion of ≥1 cranial sutures; asymmetric skull + palpable ridge along suture (unlike molding). Multiple sutures → syndromic (Crouzon, Apert). Surgery may be needed to allow brain growth.
4.2 Face — Syndromes

Down syndrome: characteristic facies may be subtle at birth; look for pronounced hypotonia, flat occiput, short neck, bilateral single palmar creases, sandal gap (45% with T21 have single palmar crease; bilateral <1% in chromosomally normal infants)
Asymmetric crying facies: normal forehead wrinkling + eye closure → congenital absence of depressor anguli oris muscle; association with CHD
Facial palsy: peripheral: cannot wrinkle forehead or close eye. Most traumatic; resolve weeks. If severe → neurosurgical consultation.
4.3 Eyes

Red Reflex (Fundal Light Reflex) — Perform Before Every Discharge
Technique: ophthalmoscope 8 inches away; both eyes together ideally; darken room or raise head 45°. Normal: red/orange glow in dark-skinned infants more yellow but vessels visible. Abnormal → immediate ophthalmology referral: (1) absent reflex = congenital cataract (most common preventable childhood blindness); (2) white pupillary reflex (leukocoria) = retinoblastoma; (3) cloudy/enlarged cornea = congenital glaucoma/corneal dystrophy. If unable to perform at first exam → DOCUMENT and complete before discharge. AAP: refer if family hx significant eye disease regardless of exam.

Coloboma: defect of lid/cornea/iris/retina/optic disc. Always inferior aspect for visible defects; complete coloboma → CHARGE syndrome workup (Coloboma, Heart, Atresia choanae, Restriction of growth, GU, Ear).
Subconjunctival hemorrhage: delivery pressure; benign; resolves 1–2 weeks

Gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum: ocular emergency — rapid progression to corneal ulceration/perforation. Purulent discharge + swollen lids day 1–4. Chlamydia: day 5–14; not prevented by topical antibiotics.
4.4 Ears
Normal position: top 1/3 of ear level with inner–outer canthus line; axis within 30° of vertical. Low-set/rotated = syndromic.

Preauricular tag: arrange hearing screen if universal screening not available. If with other dysmorphic features, renal anomalies, maternal GDM hx → renal US. Isolated tag/minor ear anomaly → renal US NOT recommended (updated guideline). Microtia → renal US recommended (may have severe renal anomaly).
Positional ear deformities (normal cartilage, abnormal architecture): may resolve; early neonatal splinting recommended to avoid surgery
4.5 Mouth and Palate

Cleft palate: must visualize entire palate including uvula with flashlight + tongue depression. Palpate hard palate entire length for submucous cleft — cannot be seen visually. Robin sequence: micrognathia + glossoptosis + U-shaped posterior cleft palate → airway obstruction.
Ankyloglossia: evaluate latch + milk transfer + maternal pain BEFORE diagnosing. Lingual frenulum not always pathologic. Upper lip frenum almost never impedes latch.
Epstein pearls: white cysts midline palate; benign. Natal teeth: often poorly attached; may be removed if loose (not permanent dentition).
4.6 Neck
Redundant posterior neck skin + flat occiput → Down syndrome. Webbed neck → Turner syndrome ± lymphedema of feet
Cystic hygroma: soft fluctuant swelling in posterior triangle; transilluminates
Palpate clavicles for crepitus/step-off — especially after shoulder dystocia. Clavicle fracture = supportive only; immobilize flexed arm in T-shirt; heals 10–14 days.
5. Cardiorespiratory Assessment
5.1 Breathing
Normal respiratory rate: 40–60 breaths/min. Normal: gentle abdominal excursions. Periodic breathing = pauses up to 10s between regular periods — normal term infant.
Respiratory distress signs: RR >60; retractions; nasal flaring; grunting → SpO₂ monitor + CXR if persistent. Pectus excavatum/carinatum: may need attention later in childhood.
5.2 Heart — Murmurs

Innocent vs Significant Neonatal Murmur — Key Features
Innocent murmur: soft grade 1–2/6 at left sternal edge; no clicks; no parasternal heave; normal pulses + normal vitals + normal exam. Origins: PDA, tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary artery bifurcation turbulence. Most disappear in first 3 months. Significant murmur features: pansystolic; ≥grade 3/6; harsh quality; upper left sternal edge; abnormal second heart sound; femoral pulses difficult to feel; abnormal vitals. Heart murmurs: 2% of newborns at routine exam. Normal exam does NOT exclude CHD: VSD may be silent at birth (equal L/R pressures); duct-dependent lesions silent until ductus closes days–weeks later.
CHD prevalence: 6–8/1000 live births; critical CHD: 1/1000 (Oster Pediatrics 2013). Any significant or uncertain murmur → cardiology or echo referral.

Femoral pulses: diminished = coarctation of aorta. If ductus patent, femoral pulses may feel normal even with coarctation. ΔBP upper vs lower >20 mmHg = significant. Postductal SpO₂ may be low. If ductaldependent lesion suspected → urgent cardiac consult.
5.3 Critical CHD Screening — Pulse Oximetry Protocol

AAP Critical CHD Screening 2020 Update (Martin 2020 Pediatrics)
Perform at ≥24h of life (or before discharge if <24h stay): right hand (preductal) AND either foot (postductal). Fail criteria (any one): SpO₂ <94% in either extremity; OR >4% absolute discordance between right hand and foot (2020 update removed the prior ≥3% criterion and reduced repeat testing to once). Pass: both ≥95% with ≤3% discordance. Swedish RCT (Granelli BMJ 2009, n=39,821): impact of screening on ductaldependent CHD detection: sensitivity ~70% for critical CHD missed by clinical exam alone.
6. Abdomen and Genitalia
6.1 Abdomen
Liver: normally 1–2 cm below right costal margin. Spleen tip occasionally palpable. Easily palpable kidneys or enlarged bladder in male → urgent US for urinary outflow obstruction.
Umbilical hernia: common in Black infants; resolve first few years; no treatment. Inguinal hernia: 4% term; 13% <33 wk; controversial timing of repair — current evidence lacking (Sulkowski 2015).

Single umbilical artery (SUA): 0.3% of newborns (Sun 2017). Associated: perinatal morbidity/mortality, chromosomal anomalies, GU + cardiovascular malformations. Renal US only if SUA + other anomaly on exam; isolated SUA: yield from renal US low (2–10% with asymptomatic mild/transient renal anomalies; reduced further by routine prenatal US)
6.2 Male Genitalia

Hypospadias: urethral meatus ventral to normal position (glans → shaft → perineum). Ventral chordee may coexist. DO NOT CIRCUMCISE: foreskin needed for surgical repair. Glanular without chordee may not need surgery; severe forms require correction.
Micropenis: stretched penile length <2 cm (pubic ramus to tip) → congenital hypopituitarism workup. Buried/webbed penis ≠ micropenis.

Undescended testes: 4% term at birth; 1% at 3 months. Refer to pediatric surgery/urology by 6 months → orchiopexy by 6–12 months (↓ infertility risk + testicular cancer risk, Chan 2014). Both undescended → evaluate for virilized female (CAH) or DSD.

Hydrocele: fluctuant, transilluminates; resolves spontaneously. Tense/hard testis → neonatal torsion → urgent pediatric surgery consult. Prenatal torsion: hard black nontender mass (may have infarcted); surgical fixation of contralateral testis often recommended.
6.3 Female Genitalia
Clitoris + labia minora prominent if preterm; covered by labia majora at term. Exposed clitoris at ≥39 wk → virilization or intersex. Cliteromegaly: width >5 mm, length >6 mm, hood >12 mm.
Imperforate hymen: bulging central membrane → inspect vaginal opening. White vaginal discharge day 1–3 → small bleeding day 3–5: normal (maternal hormone withdrawal)
Anus: distance vagina/scrotum to anus <1 cm + delayed meconium → anteriorly placed anus. Confirm patency + normal tone. Meconium + urine: both within 24h in most term newborns
7. Limbs, Hips, and Back
7.1 Limbs, Hands, and Feet
Polydactyly (ulnar): usually familial + isolated; connected by thin pedicle. Ligation of ulnar digit → stump/painful neuroma — surgical removal preferred (Goebel 2022)
Radial polydactyly: well-developed digit; wait for hand use assessment before removal. Absent/abnormal digits/rays → syndrome evaluation.

Brachial plexus palsy: lack of active movement; passive not restricted. Erb palsy (C5–C6±C7): shoulder dystocia; arm adducted/internally rotated/pronated/wrist flexed = 'waiter's tip'. Klumpke (C8-T1): 'claw hand'. Daily PT; if not recovered by 1 month → surgical center referral (Smith JAMA Pediatr 2018).
Clavicle fracture: crepitus over clavicle; ↓ movement affected arm. Treat: immobilize in undersized T-shirt; heals 10–14 days.

Positional talipes (normal foot size, passively correct past midline): PT exercises; resolves spontaneously. True clubfoot (talipes equinovarus): fixed plantar flexion + inversion; does NOT correct past midline. Early orthopedic referral; Ponseti casting (Elbaum 2022)
7.2 Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH)

Ortolani + Barlow Maneuvers — Technique and Interpretation
Prerequisite: infant supine, flat firm surface, completely relaxed (crying = false-negative). Gentle to avoid posterior acetabular lip damage. Ortolani: hips+knees 90°; stabilize pelvis; index+middle finger over greater trochanter, thumb along medial thigh; abduct + gentle upward leverage on trochanter → dislocated hip returns with clunk into acetabulum (diagnostic). Barlow: same position; gently adduct + posterior pressure → dislocatable femoral head slides out posteriorly. Ortolani = diagnostic; Barlow abnormality may resolve. Both → orthopedic referral + US at 6 weeks. After 3–4 months → use limited abduction as primary sign (infant gains strength to resist Ort/Barl).

rows: ↑ DDH — screen with US at 6 weeks

blocks.text: Most abnormal neonatal hip exams resolve by 4 weeks without intervention. Screening US at 6 weeks for risk factors (Shaw Pediatrics 2016; Mulpuri JBJS 2015)
7.3 Back and Spine

Prone position → expose full back. Inspect for: fixed curvature (elevation of rib section), midline defects, neurocutaneous markers (nevus, hemangioma, hair tuft along spine → may indicate vertebral/spinal cord anomaly → US/MRI).

Sacrococcygeal dimple: small blind-ending within 2 cm of posterior anal verge = harmless. Unusually large, no visible base, above gluteal cleft → investigate for intraspinal extension + meningitis risk. Spina bifida (often prenatal Dx) → neurosurgical referral.
8. Neurologic Examination
8.1 Level of Alertness and States
Normal newborn states: deep sleep, active/light sleep, drowsy, quiet alert, active alert, crying (Prechtl 1964). Optimal exam: quiet alert state
Abnormal levels: lethargy/stupor/coma → HIE, metabolic, structural brain disorder. Hyperalert + high-pitched cry → cerebral irritation. Excessive cry + hyperalert → drug withdrawal.
8.2 Cranial Nerve Assessment

rows: Fix on face/red ball 25 cm; pupillary light reflex
Doll's eye reflex (head turns → eyes deviate opposite); tracking; pupillary reflex (CN III)
Corneal/nasal motor response; suck + lip seal
OAE or AABR; response to mother's voice; Moro (vestibular)
Sucking + swallowing; gag; quality of cry
Tongue position + bulk; fasciculations = SMA concern

blocks.text: Tongue fasciculations: random wormlike movements at lateral tongue margins → spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) must be distinguished from normal rhythmic tremors with protrusion during crying.
8.3 Muscle Tone Assessment

Four Tone Assessment Techniques — Term Infant
Pull to sit: demonstrates hand grasp strength, arm tone, head lag (some delay normal → warn parents before doing). Axillary suspension: hands under arms + head support; normal = flexes+extends legs, feels secure. Hypotonic = axillary slippage; legs may not flex. Prone: arms flexed, legs partially flexed, lifts chest + briefly lifts head. Ventral suspension: hold prone over hand; normal = curves around hand, lifts head to body plane, legs flex+extend. Hypotonic = rag doll: lower spine + legs drape down. Note: hypotonia ≠ weakness — hypotonia can occur without weakness (cerebellar); weakness can occur with normal or ↑ tone (spastic paralysis).

Cortical thumb: persistently adducted thumb in tight fist with few finger movements → cerebral injury, genetic disorder, malformation. Normal infant shows varied finger movements including thumb abduction.

Neurologic maturation by GA: 26 wk: extension, absent rooting/sucking/Moro; 30 wk: brief alert, long latency; 34 wk: variable tone; 38 wk: flexion all limbs + adducted legs, vigorous suck, complete Moro (Fenichel Neonatal Neurology)
8.4 Primitive Reflexes

rows: Stroke corner of mouth; or firmly downward philtrum → lower lip
Open mouth + head turn toward cheek; cupped tongue → facilitates latch
Wavelike tongue undulation; draw finger into mouth
Lower head from 30–45° to horizontal (head only)
Symmetric arm+leg extension then flexion + cry; asymmetric → brachial plexus/clavicle Fx
Ipsilateral arm extends, contralateral arm flexes
All toes curl down and in
Linear firm pressure lateral to spine in prone
Hold upright; place sole on flat surface / dorsum against table edge
Stepping: leg flexes up then places foot; Placing: hip+knee flex; appears 36–38 wk PMA
Blinks with EACH tap (no extinction); adult/older child extincts after few taps

blocks.text: Plantar reflex: stroke lateral foot then across below 4th–5th toes → normally upgoing (Babinski positive = NORMAL in newborn). ~10% may have downgoing plantar response (Gupta 2003). Asymmetric plantar response → thorough neurologic exam
9. Gestational Age Assessment — Revised Ballard Score
9.1 When to Use
Formal Ballard unnecessary for routine exam if reliable early antenatal US + LMP available. Use when: GA unknown or discrepant with growth; SGA infant; no prenatal care.
Accuracy: ±2 weeks; tendency to overestimate in extremely premature infants. Early US combined with LMP remains gold standard (Lee Pediatrics 2017: 95% CI ±2–3 wk for clinical methods).
9.2 Revised Ballard Score (Ballard J Pediatr 1991;119:417)
6 neuromuscular criteria: posture, square window, arm recoil, popliteal angle, scarf sign, heel-to-ear
6 physical criteria: skin, lanugo, plantar creases, breast, eye/ear, genitalia
10. Health Promotion, Screening, and Prevention
10.1 Breastfeeding Promotion
Skin-to-skin BEYOND immediate delivery period; infant root+suck behaviors detectable when undisturbed (Widström 2011)
Evaluate latch, milk transfer, maternal pain. Expert lactation consultant essential — especially prematurity, multiple birth, illness.
10.2 Screening Programs

Newborn metabolic screening (blood spot): metabolic, endocrine, genetic conditions that benefit from early intervention; some present with devastating consequences within week 1 → close follow-up critical.

Universal hearing screening: standard of care USA + many countries. OAE or AABR before discharge. High-risk infants (family hx, preauricular tags, ear malformations, maternal GDM) → close monitoring if universal screening not available.
Critical CHD screening: AAP 2020 protocol (see Section 5.3). Fail → workup with echo.

Car Seat Tolerance Screening (CSTS): for preterm + SGA — maintain SpO₂ >95% in semi-upright car seat ≥1h. No newborn should be in semi-upright position for extended periods — even those who PASS CSTS may desat at another time. Car seats + swings NOT for extended infant sleep/play.
10.3 Prevention
Vitamin K: 1 mg IM at birth prevents vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB). Parent refusal → discuss risks; re-address at first exam.
Ocular prophylaxis: prevents gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum. Standard in USA. Does NOT prevent Chlamydia conjunctivitis.
Hepatitis B vaccine: given before discharge. Discuss immunization importance.
Safe sleep / SIDS prevention: back to sleep; no bed-sharing, no overheating, no parental smoking, breastfeeding. Prevention has markedly reduced sudden unexpected infant deaths.
10.4 Discharge Criteria (Benitz Pediatrics 2015)

Vital signs stable ≥12h; multiple successful consecutive feeds; urine + meconium passed; sepsis risk assessed per maternal risk factors; maternal labs checked (HBsAg, HIV, syphilis); hearing + blood spot + pulse ox screening completed (or arrangements made)
If term discharge <48h: follow-up with health care professional within 48h
11. NICU Practical — Bedside Applications
11.1 Skin Findings That Change Management
Port-wine stain on forehead: refer ophthalmology (glaucoma risk) + neurology (Sturge-Weber: intracranial lesions + seizures + abnormal neurodevelopment)
≥3 café-au-lait macules: examine axillae + groin for freckling (NF1); refer genetics if positive features or large lesion
Dermal melanocytosis: document exactly on admission paperwork — frequent confusion with bruising; child protection risk if undocumented
Diffuse liver hemangiomas: check TFT (hypothyroidism from iodothyronine deiodinase in hemangioma) → replace levothyroxine if hypothyroid
11.2 Scalp Emergency

Subgaleal hemorrhage: after vacuum delivery → monitor head circumference serially, vital signs, Hb/Hct — potential for major hemorrhage. Treat hypovolemic shock aggressively. Early recognition = preventing death.
11.3 Cardiac

Innocent murmur communication: manner of description profoundly affects parental anxiety. Use precise language: 'a normal heart sound that is common in newborns and almost always disappears in the first few months.'
Pulse oximetry fail: do NOT delay echo or cardiology consult. Ductaldependent lesion = time-sensitive; prostaglandin E1 if duct-dependent anatomy confirmed.
11.4 DDH in NICU

Breech + female → highest risk (10–13%). Exam often difficult in preterm due to immature musculature. Hip US at 6 weeks corrected age for preterm with risk factors. Prone positioning in NICU for respiratory: does NOT protect against DDH — needs prospective follow-up.
11.5 Neurologic Red Flags
Tongue fasciculations + hypotonia + weak cry + paradoxical breathing: SMA type 1 — urgent genetics/neuromuscular workup (SMN1 gene deletion); Zolgensma window (treatment before symptoms/age 2)
Absent red reflex: same-day ophthalmology consult regardless of busy schedule; congenital cataract = preventable blindness
Asymmetric Moro: brachial plexus palsy vs clavicle fracture; passive movement + crepitus palpation differentiates
12. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Red Reflex ต้องทำก่อน Discharge ทุกราย — เพราะ Leukocoria = Emergency
Congenital cataract เป็น most common preventable childhood blindness (AAP recommends referral for any family hx of significant eye disease). Leukocoria (white reflex) = retinoblastoma จนกว่าจะพิสูจน์อย่างอื่น — urgent ophthalmology same day. ถ้าทำไม่ได้ตอนแรก (ointment ปิด ตา, บวม) → document + redo ก่อน discharge. ห้าม discharge โดยไม่มี red reflex confirmed.

PEARL 2 — Subgaleal Hemorrhage: Boggy Head + Wave Sign = Hemorrhagic Emergency
Vacuum delivery + coagulopathy = major risk factors. Subgaleal space จุ blood ได้มากกว่า total blood volume ของทารก — hypovolemic shock เป็น real risk. Signs: boggy scalp, fluid wave ข้ามกลางศีรษะ, anterior ear displacement. Monitor: HC serial (every 2–4h), Hb/Hct, HR/BP. Transfuse early; FFP if coagulopathy. ต่างจาก cephalhematoma (confined, fluctuant, within suture) ตรงที่ subgaleal crosses sutures + unstable vitals.

PEARL 3 — Critical CHD Silent ใน Delivery Room จนกว่า Ductus จะปิด
Normal newborn exam (no murmur, pink) ไม่ exclude ductaldependent lesion — pulmonary atresia, hypoplastic left heart, coarctation อาจไม่แสดงอาการจนกว่า ductus closes (day 2–7). AAP 2020 pulse ox protocol: SpO₂ <94% either extremity OR >4% discordance R hand vs foot → workup. ถ้าสงสัย → PGE₁ IV ก่อน echo ถ้า unstable (0.05–0.1 mcg/kg/min); urgent cardiology. 'The exam was normal' ≠ 'CHD excluded.'

PEARL 4 — DDH: Ortolani Clunk ต้องฝึกหน้ากายวิภาค ไม่ใช่แค่อ่านหนังสือ
True DDH: 1/1000 infants — clunk ต้องสัมผัสให้เป็น; ไม่สามารถเรียนรู้จากภาพหรือ description เท่านั้น. ทารกต้องผ่อนคลายสมบูรณ์ — crying = false negative. Barlow abnormality อาจ resolve เอง แต่ Ortolani clunk = positive diagnosis. Risk factors: female + breech = 10–13%; refer ทั้งคู่สำหรับ US at 6 wk. Asymmetric gluteal creases + limited abduction = secondary signs ที่สำคัญ >3–4 months เมื่อ Ort/Barl difficult to interpret.

PEARL 5 — Tongue Fasciculations ≠ Tongue Tremors: SMA หรือ Normal?
SMA type 1: random wormlike movements best seen at lateral tongue margins (อาจเห็นได้ชัดขณะ tongue สงบ). ต่างจาก tremors: rhythmic movements เห็นชัดเมื่อ tongue โผล่ออกมาระหว่างร้องไห้ (normal). Red flags for SMA: hypotonia + weak cry + paradoxical breathing (chest sinks in during inspiration) + fasciculations + absent DTRs. Newborn screen ใน Thailand ยังไม่ครอบคลุม SMN1 ทุกที่ — clinical suspicion + SMN1 MLPA ต้องสั่งถ้าสงสัย. เวลาสำคัญ: treatment ก่อน irreversible motor neuron loss.
13. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Widström AM et al Acta Paediatr 2011;100:79: skin-to-skin behavior sequence leading to breast — 9 stages; support for uninterrupted skin-to-skin first hour of life
Moore ER et al Cochrane 2016;(11):CD003519: early skin-to-skin contact → ↑ breastfeeding duration + cardiorespiratory stability during transition
Flaherman VJ et al Pediatrics 2015;135:e16: early weight loss nomograms exclusively breastfed newborns; NEWT.org tool; median 7% loss by 48h
Oumer M et al Public Health Rev 2021;42:1604044: anterior fontanelle size meta-analysis: pooled mean 2.58 cm; regional variation (Africa 3.15 vs Europe 2.01 cm)
Ballard JL et al J Pediatr 1991;119:417: revised Ballard score including extremely premature infants; 6 physical + 6 neurologic criteria; accuracy ±2 weeks
Lee ACC et al Pediatrics 2017;140:e20171423: systematic review gestational age assessment: 95% CI ±2–3 wk for clinical methods; tendency to underestimate growth-restricted infants
Waelchli R et al Br J Dermatol 2014;171:861: new vascular classification of port-wine stains; predicts Sturge-Weber risk based on distribution along V branches
Wassef M et al Pediatrics 2015;136:196: ISSVA vascular anomalies classification: malformations vs tumors — guidance for terminology and diagnosis
Roth DA et al Pediatrics 2008;122:884: preauricular skin tags/pits associated with permanent hearing impairment in newborns → hearing screen indication
Desphande SA & Watson H Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal 2006;91:F29: isolated minor ear anomaly without other risk factors: renal US NOT required
Fenster ME & Hokanson JS Congenit Heart Dis 2018;13:771: heart murmurs in normal newborn nursery: 2% prevalence at routine examination
Oster ME et al Pediatrics 2013;131:e1502: temporal trends survival among infants with critical CHD; 6–8/1000 live births with CHD; 1/1000 critical CHD
Granelli AW et al BMJ 2009;338:a3037: pulse oximetry screening n=39,821: impact on detection ductaldependent CHD — sensitivity ~70% for critical CHD not identified clinically
Martin GR et al Pediatrics 2020;146:e20191650: updated AAP strategies pulse oximetry screening: SpO₂ <94% or >4% discordance → fail; removed prior <95%/3% criteria
Shaw BA & Segal LS (AAP) Pediatrics 2016;138:e20163107: DDH evaluation and referral guideline: US screening at 6 wk for risk factors (female, breech, family hx)
Chan E et al Pediatr Surg Int 2014;30:87: systematic review ideal timing orchiopexy: complete by 6–12 months to preserve testicular function + reduce cancer risk
Smith BW et al JAMA Pediatr 2018;172:585: updated management neonatal brachial plexus palsy: PT first; if not recovered by 1 month → surgical center evaluation
Goebel GJ et al J Pediatr Orthop 2022;42:e466: long-term outcomes type B ulnar polydactyly: ligation → neuromas/stumps; surgical removal preferred
Sulkowski JP et al J Pediatr Surg 2015;50:171: timing neonatal inguinal hernia repair: definitive data lacking; controversial
Gupta A & Gupta P J Paediatr Child Health 2003;39:349: neonatal plantar response: ~10% normal newborns may have downgoing plantar; symmetry = key finding
Benitz W (AAP) Pediatrics 2015;135:948: hospital stay criteria healthy term newborns: discharge checklist including vital signs, feeds, voiding, screening
14. Self-Check Q&A
Q: ทารกหลังคลอด routine exam: ลำดับการตรวจควรเป็นอย่างไร และทำไม?

ตอบ: ลำดับ flexible แต่ strategy คือ 'opportunistic first': ขณะทารกสงบ → ฟัง heart, ตรวจ eyes, ตรวจ femoral pulses, hip exam ก่อน. จากนั้น general observation ขณะ undress → remove diaper → ตรวจ genitalia → prone ตรวจ back + spine + tone. เหตุผล: crying = false-negative สำหรับ heart murmur + hip exam. ทำ exam ที่ mother's bedside เสมอ — ไม่รบกวน nursing dyad.
Q: แยก caput succedaneum, cephalhematoma, subgaleal hemorrhage: ลักษณะและ urgency?

ตอบ: Caput: crosses sutures, pitting edema, resolves 1 wk. Cephalhematoma: confined within sutures, fluctuant, parietal bone, resolves 2–3 wk, contributes to jaundice. Subgaleal: crosses all sutures (galea space), boggy, 'wave sign', anterior ear displacement. EMERGENCY: major blood loss → hypovolemic shock. Post-vacuum + coagulopathy = high risk. Monitor HC serial + Hb/Hct + VS hourly.
Q: Red reflex: technique ที่ถูกต้องและ abnormal findings ที่ต้อง refer ทันที?

ตอบ: Technique: ophthalmoscope 8 inches from eyes; darken room; both eyes together (ตรวจ comparison). Dark-skinned infants → more yellow tint but vessels visible. Abnormal → immediate ophthalmology: absent reflex (congenital cataract); leukocoria (white pupil) = retinoblastoma; hazy/enlarged cornea = glaucoma. ถ้าทำไม่ได้ตอนแรก → document → ทำก่อน discharge.
Q: Innocent murmur vs significant murmur: clinical features ที่แยก?

ตอบ: Innocent: soft 1–2/6, left sternal edge, no clicks, no heave, normal femoral pulses + normal vitals + normal exam. Significant: pansystolic, ≥3/6, harsh, upper left sternal edge, abnormal S2, femoral pulses weak/absent (coarctation). สำคัญ: normal exam ≠ no CHD — ductaldependent lesions silent จนกว่า ductus closes.
Q: DDH exam: Ortolani vs Barlow — technique, interpretation, risk factors?

ตอบ: Ortolani: abduct + upward leverage trochanter → dislocated hip returns with clunk (diagnostic). Barlow: adduct + posterior pressure → dislocatable hip slides out. Infant must be relaxed. Both → orthopedic referral + hip US at 6 wk. Risk: female + breech 3rd trimester = 10–13%; family hx; clubfoot; neuromuscular. Barlow may resolve; Ortolani clunk = confirmed DDH.
Q: Undescended testis: ทำไม timing ของ orchiopexy สำคัญ?

ตอบ: Orchiopexy ก่อน 6–12 เดือน: ↓ infertility risk (spermatogonia sensitive to ↑ temperature) + ↓ testicular cancer risk (3–5x ↑ risk retained testis). Refer by 6 months (Chan Pediatr Surg Int 2014). Both undescended → evaluate: virilized female with CAH or DSD (karyotype + 17-OHP + testosterone + AMH). 4% term at birth; 1% at 3 months (most self-descend by 3 months).
Q: Tongue fasciculations ในทารกแรกเกิด: เมื่อไหร่ต้อง workup SMA?

ตอบ: SMA type 1 red flags: hypotonia + weak cry + paradoxical breathing (chest sinks on inspiration) + absent DTRs + tongue fasciculations at rest (random wormlike movements lateral tongue). Distinguish from normal tremors (rhythmic, prominent during crying). Workup: SMN1 gene deletion (MLPA); CK; EMG if needed. เวลาสำคัญ: treatment ก่อน motor neuron loss — Zolgensma/Spinraza window.
Q: Critical CHD screening: AAP 2020 fail criteria คืออะไร และต้องทำอะไรต่อ?

ตอบ: Fail: SpO₂ <94% in either extremity (right hand or either foot) OR >4% absolute discordance between right hand and foot on measurement (2020: reduced to 1 repeat, removed old 3% criterion). Action: urgent echo ± cardiology consult. If ductaldependent lesion confirmed or suspected → PGE₁ IV 0.05–0.1 mcg/kg/min immediately. Screen at ≥24h (or before early discharge).
Q: Hypospadias: ทำไม circumcision เป็น contraindication?

ตอบ: Foreskin เป็น surgical flap ที่ใช้ repair hypospadias ± chordee. ถ้า circumcise → ไม่มี tissue สำหรับ urethroplasty → ต้อง skin graft จากที่อื่น (มี morbidity มากกว่า). Glanular hypospadias without chordee มักไม่ต้อง surgery; severe forms (penoscrotal/perineal) ต้องแก้ไข. Document ทุกราย + inform parents: defer circumcision จนกว่า urologist เห็น.

Day 125 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.28
บทต่อไป: Chapter 28 — Birth Injuries. เตรียมตัว: classification of birth injuries (intracranial hemorrhage, spinal cord, brachial plexus, skeletal, soft tissue); mechanisms: prolonged labor, shoulder dystocia, vacuum/forceps; clinical presentation + imaging; management (subdural vs epidural vs subarachnoid); facial nerve vs brachial plexus palsy (Erb vs Klumpke): mechanism + recovery timeline; clavicle + humerus + femur fractures in delivery; neonatal spinal cord injury.
